steps.title: Extract vertex and opening direction from vertex form
steps.content: The equation $y = -3(x-18)^2 + 15$ is in vertex form $y = a(x-h)^2 + k$. The vertex is $(h, k) = (18, 15)$, and since $a = -3 < 0$, the parabola opens downward with a maximum value of $y = 15$.

steps.title: Compare the line to the parabola's range
steps.content: The line $y = 18$ is horizontal. Since the parabola reaches a maximum of only 15, and the line is at 18, the line lies entirely above the parabola and they cannot intersect. That matches choice D — D is correct.

steps.title: Use the source rationale
steps.content: Choice D is correct. A point $\left(x,y\right)$ is a solution to a system of equations if it lies on the graphs of both equations in the xy-plane. In other words, a solution to a system of equations is a point $\left(x,y\right)$ at which the graphs intersect. It's given that the first equation is $y=18$. Substituting $18$ for $y$ in the second equation yields $18=-3{\left(x-18\right)}^{2}+15$. Subtracting $15$ from each side of this equation yields $3=-3{\left(x-18\right)}^{2}$. Dividing each side of this equation by $-3$ yields $-1={\left(x-18\right)}^{2}$. Since the square of a real number is at least $0$, this equation can't have any real solutions. Therefore, the graphs of the equations intersect at zero points.

steps.title: Check the choices
steps.content: Alternate approach: The graph of the second equation is a parabola that opens downward and has a vertex at $\left(18,15\right)$. Therefore, the maximum value of this parabola occurs when $y=15$. The graph of the first equation is a horizontal line at $18$ on the y-axis, or $y=18$. Since $18$ is greater than $15$, or the horizontal line is above the vertex of the parabola, the graphs of these equations intersect at zero points.

steps.title: Check the choices
steps.content: Choice A is incorrect. The graph of $y=15$, not $y=18$, and the graph of the second equation intersect at exactly one point.

steps.title: Check the choices
steps.content: Choice B is incorrect. The graph of any horizontal line such that the value of $y$ is less than $15$, not greater than $15$, and the graph of the second equation intersect at exactly two points.

steps.title: Check the choices
steps.content: Choice C is incorrect and may result from conceptual or calculation errors.
